definition: A sibyl is a prophetess of antiquity — one of the inspired women who uttered oracles in a divine frenzy, most famously the Cumaean Sibyl who guided Aeneas to the underworld. Where a *seer* merely sees and a *soothsayer* speaks truth, the sibyl prophesies possessed, her scattered utterances gathered into books and consulted for centuries.
history: The word comes through Latin *sibylla* from Greek *síbylla*, 'prophetess'. Antiquity's freelance oracles, the Sibyls left behind the Sibylline books — so prized that when Tarquin burned all but three, Rome paid a fortune for what remained (Reuchlin, *Augenspiegel*). Their great turn was Christian: Lactantius grouped 'Hermes with the Sibyl' among the pagan witnesses to Christ (Litwa, *Hermetica II*), and so the Sibyls were carved beside the prophets in cathedral choir-stalls and floor-mosaics — pagan prophetesses folded into the church's own prehistory (Kieckhefer, *Magic in the Middle Ages*).

def_sources.label: Richard Kieckhefer, Magic in the Middle Ages

etymology.note: The Sibyls were antiquity’s freelance prophetesses; the Cumaean Sibyl guided Aeneas to the underworld.
etymology.hook: The women who out-ranked the oracles.

flavor_snippets.html: The first Star <mark>Sibyl</mark> gained the powers of the stars and moon at Full Moon Fountain. She found the stars of hope from within the darkness, and led Windurst to glory.
